value: Differential equation
An equation containing the derivatives of one or more unknown functions (or dependent variables), with respect to one or more independent variables
j
Ordinary differential equation (ODE)
A differential equation that contains only ordinary derivatives of one or more unknown functions with respect to a single independent variable
y
Partial differential equations (PDE)
An equation involving partial derivatives of one or more unknown functions of two or more independent variables
k
Order of a differential equation
The order of the highest derivative in the equation
Normal Form
j
Linear ODE
j
Solution of an ODE

Any function [$]\phi[/$], defined on an interval [$]I[/$] and posessing at least [$]n[/$] derivatives that are continuous on [$]I[/$], which when substituted into an [$]n^{th}[/$]-order ordinary differential equation reduces the equation to an identity, is said to be a solution of the equation on the interval.
Trivial solution
A solution of a differential equation that is identically zero on an interval [$]I[/$]
j
Explicit solution
A solution in which the dependent variable is expressed solely in terms of the independent variable and constants
j
Implicit solution of an ODE

A relation [$]G(x, y)=0[/$] of an ordinary differential equation on an interval [$]I[/$], provided that there exists at leastone function [$]\phi[/$] that satisfies the relation as well as the differential equation on [$]I[/$]
N-parameter family of solutions definition
A solution with n arbitrary constants
An nth order differential equation has an {{c1::n}}-parameter family of solutions.
Particular solution
A solution of a differential equation that is free of arbitrary parameters
j
Singular solution
A solution that cannot be obtained by specializing any of the parameters of a family of solutions
j
System of ordinary differential equations
Two or more equations involving the derivatives of two or more unknown functions of a single independent variable
j
General solution
An n-parameter family of solutions from which every solution of an nth-order ODE on an interval I can be obtained by appropriate choices of the parameters [$]c_i[/$]
Existence of a Unique Solution for Initial Value Problems

Let [$]a_n(x), a_{n-1}(x),...,a_1(x), a_0(x)[/$] and [$]g(x)[/$] be continuous on an interval [$]I[/$] and let [$]a_n(x)\neq 0[/$] for every[$]x[/$] in this interval. If [$]x=x_0[/$] is any point on this interval, then a solution [$]y(x)[/$] of the initial-value problem exists on the interval and is unique.
j
Homogeneous equation
A linear nth order differential equation of the form [$]a_n(x)\frac{d^ny}{dx^n}+a_{n-1}(x)\frac{d^{n-1}y}{dx^{n-1}}+...+a_1(x)\frac{dy}{dx}+a_0(x)y=0[/$]
j
Nonhomogeneous equation
A linear nth order differential equation of the form [$]a_n(x)\frac{d^ny}{dx^n}+a_{n-1}(x)\frac{d^{n-1}y}{dx^{n-1}}+...+a_1(x)\frac{dy}{dx}+a_0(x)y=g(x)[/$] where [$]g(x)[/$] is not identically zero.
f
In [$]\frac{dy}{dx}=Dy[/$], D is the {{c1::differential operator}} because it transforms a differentiable function into another function.
Nth order differential operator (polynomial operator)
j
Superposition Principle- Homogeneous Equations

Let y1, y2,..., yk be solutions of the homogeneous nth-order differential equation on an interval I. Then the linear combination
[$]y=c_1y_1(x)+c_2y_2(x)+...+c_ky_k(x)[/$], 
where the ci, i = 1, 2,..., k are arbitrary constants, is also a solution on the interval.
A constant multiple {{c1::[$]y=c_1y_1(x)[/$]}} of a solution y<sub>1</sub>(x) of a homogeneous linear differential equation is also a solution
A homogeneous linear differential equation always posesses the {{c1::trivial}} solution {{c2::y=0}}.

A set of functions [$]f_1(x), f_2(x),...,f_n(x)[/$] is said to be {{c1::linearly dependent}} on an interval I if there exists constants [$]c_1, c_2,...c_n[/$], not all zero, such that
[$]c_1f_1(x)+c_2f_2(x)+...+c_nf_n(x)=0[/$]
for every x in the interval. If the set of functions is not linearly dependent of the interval, it is said to be {{c2::linearly independent}}.
flip
Wronskian

[latex] 

Suppose each of the functions $f_1(x), f_2(x),...,f_n(x)$ possesses at least $n-1$ derivatives. The determinant

$W(f_1,f_2,...,f_n)=$ the matrix of $f_1, f_2, ..., f_n$, with each of the next rows being its n-1 derivatives (totaling n rows)

[/latex]

Let [$]y_1, y_2,...,y_n[/$] be n solutions of the homogeneous linear nth-order differential equaion on an interval I. Then the set of solutions is linearly independent on I if and only if {{c1::[$]W(y_1, y_2,...y_n)\neq 0[/$] for every x in the interval}}.
Fundamental set of solutions
Any set [$]y_1, y_2,...,y_n[/$] of n linearly independent solutions of the homogeneous linear nth-order differential equaion on an interval I.
k
Existence of a fundamental set
There exists a fundamental set of solutions for the homogeneous linear nth-order differential equation on an interval I.
General Solution- Homogeneous Linear Equations

Let [$]y_1, y_2,...,y_n[/$] be a fundamental set of solutions of the homogeneous linear nth-order differential equation on an interval I. Then the general solution of the equation on the interval is
[$]y=c_1y_1(x)+c_2y_2(x)+...+c_ny_n(x)[/$],
where ci, i = 1, 2,...,n are arbitrary constants
General Solution- Nonhomogeneous Equations

Let [$]y_p[/$] be any particular solution of the nonhomogeneous linear nth-order differential equation on an interval I, and let [$]y_1, y_2,...,y_n[/$] be a fundamental set of solutions of the associated homogeneous differential equation on I. Then the general solution of the equation on the interval is
[$]y=c_1y_1(x)+c_2y_2(x)+...+ c_ny_n(x)+y_p[/$],
where ci, i = 1, 2,...,n are arbitrary constants.
Complementary function
For a nonhomogeneous equation, the linear combination [$]y_c(x)=c_1y_1(x) + c_2y_2(x)+...+c_ny_n(x)[/$], which is the general solution for a homogeneous function.
Simplified general solution for nonhomogeneous equations
y = complementary fucntion + any particular solution = y<sub>c</sub> + y<sub>p</sub><sub>.</sub>

Let [$]y_{p_1},y_{p_2},...,y_{p_k}[/$] be k particular solutions of the nonhomogeneous linear nth-order differential equation on an interval I corresponding, in turn, to k distinct functions [$]g_1,g_2,...,g_k[/$]. That is, suppose [$]y_{p_1}[/$] denotes a particular solution of the corresponding differential equation

[$]a_n(x)y^{(n)}+a_{n-1}(x)y^{(n-1)}+...+a_1(x)y'+a_0(x)y=g_i(x)[/$],

where i = 1, 2,...,k. Then

{{c1::[$]y_p=y_{p_1}(x)+y_{p_2}(x)+...+y_{p_k}(x)[/$]}}

is a particular solution of

{{c2::[$]a_n(x)y^{(n)} + a_{n-1}(x)y^{(n-1)}+...+a_1(x)y'+a_0(x)y=g_1(x) + g_2(x) +...+g_k(x)[/$]}}
Superposition principle- nonhomogeneous equations

A dynamical system whose rule or mathematical model is a linear nth-order differential equation

[$]a_n(t)y^{(n)}+a_{n-1}(t)y^{(n-1)}+...+a_1(t)y'+a_0(t)y=g(t)[/$]

is said to be an nth-order {{c1::linear system}}. The n time-dependent functions [$]y(t), y'(t)...y^{(n-1)}(t)[/$] are the {{c2::state variables}} of the system. The  is variously called the {{c3::input function, forcing function, or the excitation function}}. A solution y(t) of the differential equation is said to be the output or response of the system.
Modelling Process

1. Express assumptions and hypotheses in terms of differential equations
2. Solve the differential equaitons
3. Display the predictions of the model (ie graphically)
4. Check model predictions with known facts
5. If neccesary, alster assumptions or increase resolution of model
Separable Equation

A first-order differential equation of the form

[$]\frac{dy}{dx}=g(x)h(y)[/$]
j
Definition of a mathematical model
The mathematical description of a system of phenomenon
Initial conditions
In an nth-order initial value problem, the values of y(x) and its first n-1 derivatives at [$]x_0,y(x_0)=y_0,y'(x_0)=y_1,...,y^{(n-1)}(x_0)=y_{n-1}[/$]
j
Partial differential equation (PDE)
An equation involving partial derivatives of one or more unknown functions of two or more independent variables
j
Proportional

[latex]
If two quantities u and v are proportional, we write u $\propto$ v. This means that one quantity is a constant multiple of the other: 
u = kv
[/latex]
Singular points
Values of x for which a<sub>1</sub>(x)=0
j
Existence of a unique solution for a first-order DE

[latex]Let R be a rectangular region in the xy-plane defined by $a \leq x \leq b, c \leq y \leq d$ that contains the point $(x_0,y_0)$ in its interior.\\
If $f(x,y)$ and $\frac{\partial f}{\partial y}$ are continuous on R, then there exists some interval $I_0$: $(x_0-h,x_0+h), h > 0$, contained in [a, b], and a unique function y(x), defined on $I_0$, that is a solution of the initial-value problem[/latex]
Standard form of a first-order differential equation
[$]\frac{dy}{dx} + P(x)y = f(x)[/$]
j
Rate of change of amount of salt in tank

[latex]
$\frac{dA}{dt}=($input rate of salt$) - ($output rate of salt$) = R_{in}-R_{out}$\\
$\frac{dA}{dt}=$ change in amount of salt in tank\\
$A(t)$ = amount of salt in tank at time $t$.
[/latex]
Exact equation

[latex] A first-order differential equation of the form

\[M(x,y)dx+N(x,y)dy=0\],\\

if the expression on the left-hand side is an exact differential.
[/latex]
j
State of the system
In a model involving the variable time t, the values of the dependent variable(s) for appropriate values of t describe the system in the past, present, and future.
j
Solution curve
The graph of a solution [$]\rho[/$] of an ODE
j
Boundary conditions
[latex]In an nth-order boundary-value problem, the values of $y(x)$ or its derivatives at two or more points $x_0, x_1,...$[/latex]
j
Radioactive decay model

[latex]$\frac{dA}{dt} = kA$\\
$\frac{dA}{dt}$: the rate at which the nuclei of a substance decay
$A(t)$: number of nuclei remaining at time $t$
[/latex]
Newton's law of cooling

[latex]$\frac{dT}{dt}=k(T-T_m)$\\
$T(t) =$ temperature of a body at time $t$\\
$T_m =$ ambient temperature\\
$\frac{dT}{dt} =$ rate at which temperature of a body changes
[/latex]
j
Rate of disease spreading

[latex]$\frac{dx}{dt}=kxy$\\
$x(t)$ = number of people who have contracted disease\\
$y(t)$ = number of people not yet exposed\\
$\frac{dx}{dt}$ = rate at which disease spreads\\
Assume that the number of interactions is jointly proportional to $x$ and $y$.[/latex]
Population growth model
[$]\frac{dP}{dt}=kP[/$]
Criterion for an exact differential

[latex]Let $M(x,y)$ and $N(x,y)$ be continuous and have continuous first partial derivatives in a rectangular region $R$ defined by $a<x<b$, $c<y<d$. Then a necessary and sufficient condition that $M(x,y)dx + N(x,y)dy$ is an exact differential is
\[\frac{\partial M}{\partial y}= \frac{\partial N}{\partial x}\][/latex]
Transient term
[latex]A term that approaches $0$ as $x \rightarrow \infty$[/latex]
j

[latex]A differential expression $M(x,y)dx + N(x,y)dy$ is {{c1::the exact differential}} in a region $R$ of the xy-plane if it corresponds to the differential of some function $f(x,y)$ defined in $R$.[/latex]
Steps for solving a linear first-order equation

[latex]\begin{enumerate}
\item Put equation in standard form
\item Identify $P(x)$ to find the integrating factor $e^{\int P(x) dx}f(x)$
\item Multiply both sides by the integrating factor.
\begin{enumerate}
\item The left side of the equation is the derivative of the product of the integrating factor and y:
\[\frac{d}{dx}[e^{\int P(x) dx}y]= e^{\int P(x) dx}f(x)\]
\end{enumerate}
\item Integrate both sides and solve for $y$
\end{enumerate}
[/latex]
j
Height of water in a tank with a drain

[latex]$\frac{dh}{dt} = -\frac{A_h}{A_w}\sqrt{2gh}$\\
$\frac{dh}{dt}$ = change in heigh of water\\
$\sqrt{2gh}$ = velocity of water going through sharp-edged hole\\
$A_h$ = Area of hole\\
$A_w$ = Area of upper surface of water\\
$g$ = gravity\\
$h$ = height
[/latex]
j
Boundary-value problem (BVP)

[latex]On some interval $I$ containing $x_0$, the problem of solving an nth-order differential equation subject to n boundary conditions specified on $y(x)$ or its derivatives at two or more different points $x_0, x_1, x_m$[/latex]
j
Homogeneous function

[latex]
The DE $M(x,y)dx+N(x,y)dy=0$, of the $\alpha$ degree if there exists $\alpha \in R$ such that
\[M(tx,ty)=t^\alpha M(x,y) \mbox{and} N(tx,ty) = t^\alpha N(x,y)\]
[/latex]
j
Homogeneous function substitution
[$]y=ux \mbox{or} x=vy[/$]
Type of equation created after substituting in a homogeneous function
Separable DE
Nth order initial-value problem (IVP)

[latex] (on some interval $I$ containing $x_0$)\\

The problem of solving an nth-order differential equation subject to n side conditions specified at $x_0$:
\[\mbox{Solve: } \frac{d^ny}{dx^n}=f(x,y,y',...,y^{(n-1)})\]

\[\mbox{Subject to: } y(x_0) = y_0, y'(x_0) = y_1, y^{(n-1)}(x_0) = y_{n-1}\]\\

(where $y_0, y_1,...,y_n-1$ are arbitrary real constants)

[/latex]

[latex]
A differential equation of the form

{{c1::\[\frac{dy}{dx} = f(Ax+By+C)\]\\}}

can always be reduced to {{c2::a separable equation (an equation with separable variables)}} by means of the substitution

{{c3::
\[u = Ax + By + C\]\\

if $B \neq 0\]
}}
[/latex]
Reduction to separation of variables
Bernoulli's equation

[latex]
A differential equation of the form $\frac{dy}{dx} + P(x)y = f(x)y^n$ for any $n \in R$
[/latex]
j
Bernoulli's equation appropriate substitution

[latex] 
If $n /neq 0,1$ then $u = y^{1-n}$ is an appropriate substitution.\\

Remeber the $n$ comes from the $y^n$ of the equation.
[/latex]
What form does a Bernoulli equation become after substitution?
Linear equation
Definition of a differential

[latex]
If $z = f(x,y)$ is a function of two variables with continuous first partial derivatives in a region $R$ of the xy-plane, \\

then its differential is:

\[dz = \frac{/partial f}{/partial x}dx + \frac{/partial f}{/partial y}dy\]
[/latex]
What is the general solution for a homogeneous linear equation with constant coefficients and complex roots?

[latex]
$y = c_1e^{\alpha x}\cos{\beta x} + c_2e^{\alpha x}\sin{\beta x} = e^{\alpha x}(c_1 \cos{\beta x} + c_2 \sin{\beta x})$
[/latex]
What is the method of undetermined coefficients?
A method for solving non-homogeneous linear differential equations with constant coefficients
What are the steps in the method of undetermined coefficients?

[latex]
\begin{enumerate}
\item Find the solution $y_c$ to the complementary homogeneous equation
\item Guess the form of $y_p$ based on the form of $g(x)$
\item Find $y', y'',...,y^{(n)}$ for this candidate for $y_p$
\item Substitute these derivatives into the DE, and associate like-terms to set up a system of linear equations for the undetermined coefficients
\item Solve this system of linear equations
\item Put the complimentary and particular solutions together to get the general solution: $y = y_c + c_p$
\end{enumerate}
[/latex]
What is the multiplication rule for the method of undetermined coefficients?

[latex]
If any of the terps of the candidate for $y_p$ match the form of any of the terms in $y_c$, then multiply $y_p$ by $x^n$, where $n$ is the smallest natural number such that $x^n(y_p)$ no longer has any common terms with $y_c$
[/latex]
When do you use the multiplication rule for the method of undetermined coefficients?

[latex]
You use the multiplication rule if any of the terms in the candidate for $y_p$ match the form of any of the terms in $y_c$
[/latex]
What is the method of variation of parameters?
A method for solving non-homogeneous linear differential equations.
What are the steps to the variation of parameters method of solving differential equations?

[latex]
\begin{enumerate}
\item Find the complementary solution $y_c = c_1y_1 + c_2y_2$
\item Guess $y_p$ with variable parameters $y_p = u_1y_1 + u_2y_2$
\item Find the Wronskian $W$ of $y_1$ and $y_2$
\item Find $u_1$ and $u_2$ when
\[u_1 = \int \frac{-y_2 f(x)}{W}dx\]
\[u_2 = \int \frac{y_1f(x)}{W}dx\]
\item Build the general solution $y = c_1y_1 + c_2y_2 + u_1y_1 + u_2y_2$
\end{enumerate}
[/latex]
In the method of variation of parameters, what is the equations for u<sub>1</sub>?

[latex]
\[u_1 = \int \frac{-y_2 f(x)}{W} dx\]
[/latex]
In the method of variation of parameters, what is the equation for u<sub>2</sub>?

[latex]
\[u_2 = \int \frac{y_1 f(x)}{W} dx\]
[/latex]
Compare when you can use the method of undetermined coefficients and when you can use the variation of parameters

For the method of undetermined coefficients, you need to have constant coefficients and your g(x) should be one of the easy guesses in the table. One advantage, however, is that there is no integration.

For the method of variation of parameters, you can work with an equation with variable coefficients which has a hard-to-guess form of g(x). The drawback is that you may integrate a complicated formula.

[latex]
In the method of undetermined coefficients, if g(x) = {{c1::a constant}}, then $y_p$ = {{c2::$A$}}
[/latex]

[latex]
In the method of undetermined coefficients, if $g(x)$ = {{c1::$5x + 7$}}, then $y_p$ = {{c2::$Ax+B$}}
[/latex]

[latex]
In the method of undetermined coefficients, if $g(x)$ = {{c1::$3x^2 - 2$}}, then $y_p$ = {{c2::$Ax^2 + Bx + C$}}.
[/latex]

[latex]
In the method of undetermined coefficients, if $g(x)$ = {{c1::$x^3 - x -1$}}, then $y_p$ = {{c2::$Ax^3 + Bx^2 + Cx + E$}}.
[/latex]

[latex]
In the method of undetermined coefficients, if $g(x)$ = {{c1::$\sin{4x}$}}, then $y_p$ = {{c2::$A\sin{4x} + B \cos{4x}$}}.
[/latex]

[latex]
In the method of undetermined coefficients, if $g(x)$ = {{c1::$\cos{4x}$}}, then $y_p$ = {{c2::$A \sin{4x} + B\cos{4x}$}}.
[/latex]

[latex]
In the method of undetermined coefficients, if $g(x)$ = {{c1::$e^{5x}$}}, then $y_p$ = {{c2::$Ae^{5x}$}}.
[/latex]

[latex]
In the method of undetermined coefficients, if $g(x)$ = {{c1::$(9x-2)e^{5x}$}}, then $y_p$ = {{c2::$(Ax+B)e^{5x}$}}.
[/latex]

[latex]
In the method of undetermined coefficients, if $g(x)$ = {{c1::$x^2e^{5x}$}}, then $y_p$ = {{c2::$(Ax^2+Bx+C)e^{5x}$}}.
[/latex]

[latex]
In the method of undetermined coefficients, if $g(x)$ = {{c1::$e^{3x}\sin{4x}$}}, then $y_p$ = {{c2::$Ae^{3x}\sin{4x} + Be^{3x}\cos{4x}$}}.
[/latex]

[latex]
In the method of undetermined coefficients, if $g(x)$ = {{c1::$5x^2\sin{4x}$}}, then $y_p$ = {{c2::$(Ax^2+Bx+C)\sin{4x} + (Ex^2 + Fx + G)\cos{4x}$}}.
[/latex]

[latex]
In the method of undetermined coefficients, if $g(x)$ = {{c1::$xe^{3x}\cos{4x}$}}, then $y_p$ = {{c2::$(Ax+B)e^{3x}\sin{4x} + (Cx+E)e^{3x}\cos{4x}$}}.
[/latex]
How do you write the free undamped motion of a spring as a differential equation?

[latex]
$x'' + \omega^2x = 0$\\
where x'' = acceleration of the mass\\
k = spring constant\\
m = mass attached to free end of spring\\
$\omega$ = $\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$
x = displacement from equilibrium
[/latex]
What are the two forms of the solution for the differential equation for the free undamped motion of a spring?

[latex]
\begin{enumerate}
\item $x(t) =$ $c_1 \cos{\omega t} + c_2 \sin{\omega t}$
\item $x(t) = A \sin{\omega t + \phi}$
\end{enumerate}

where $A = \sqrt{c_1^2 + c_2^2}$ = amplitude\\
$\phi = \tan^{-1}{\frac{c_1}{c_2}}$ (sometimes $+\pi$) = phase angle
[/latex]
When do you add [$]\pi[/$] to the phase angle in a spring DE?

[latex]
Add $\pi$ when:
\begin{enumerate}
\item $c_1 < 0$ and $c_2 < 0$
\item $c_1 > 0$ and $c_2 < 0$
\end{enumerate}
[/latex]
What is the differential equation for the free damped motion of a spring?

[latex]
$x'' + 2\lambda x' + \omega^2 x = 0$ when\\
x'' = acceleration of mass\\
$beta$ = damping constant\\
m = mass\\
$\lambda$ = $\frac{\beta}{2m}$\\
x' = velocity of mass\\
k = spring constant\\
$\omega$ = $\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$\\
x = displacement of mass from equilibrium
[/latex]
When is a spring overdamped?

[latex]
When $\lambda^2 > \omega^2$
[/latex]
What are the types of solutions to the auxiliary equation for an overdamped spring?
The auxiliary equation has two real roots
When is a spring critically damped?

[latex]
When $\lambda^2 = \omega^2$
[/latex]
What are the types of solutions to the auxiliary equation of a critically damped spring?
The auxiliary equation has one real double root
When is a spring underdamped?

[latex]
When $\lambda^2 < \omega^2$
[/latex]
What are the solutions to the auxiliary equation of an underdamped spring?
The auxiliary equatin has two complex roots
What is the differential equation for a spring with driven motion?

[latex]
$x'' + 2\lambda x' + \omega^2 x = F(t)$ where\\

$f(t) =$ external driving force\\
$F(t) = \frac{f(t)}{m}$
[/latex]
What does Hooke's law say?

[latex] 
A spring exerts a force opposite the direction of elongation and proportional to the amount of elongation.
\[F = -ks\], where\\
k = spring constant\\
s = displacement from equilibrium
[/latex]
